old_claim.note: Iron Working provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: The Stele of the Vultures documents commanded shield-and-spear troops around 2450 BCE, providing an earlier organized-formation lineage without asserting direct transmission to Greek hoplite tactics.

source_shape.source_locator: Collection record, Description/Features and Places and Dates: dates the Stele of the Vultures to about 2450 BCE and describes multiple helmeted soldiers bearing shields and spears, led by Eannatum at the head of his troops.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The Louvre object record documents commanded shield-and-spear troops more than a millennium before the Greek hoplite phalanx.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The earlier formation is the relevant organizational ancestry for a later close-order formation. Egyptian meteoritic iron beads demonstrate metalworking, not the troop coordination that defines this node.

ontology_before: The target treated the earliest hammered iron artifacts as a direct cause of organized formation tactics.
ontology_after: The target inherits organized battlefield formation as a historical lineage while its material panoply is modeled separately.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed and locked by graph invariant 2026-07-16-organized-military-formations-hoplite-boundary: organized_warfare is present and iron_working is absent.
would_reject_if: Primary evidence establishes that the Greek close-order formation depended specifically on iron working rather than on earlier organized warfare and its own mixed-material panoply.
short_reason: Earlier commanded shielded troops are a relevant formation predecessor; meteoritic iron beads are not.
